RESOURCE ARTICLE
Affordable de novo generation of fish mitogenomes using 
amplification-  free enrichment of mitochondrial DNA and deep 
sequencing of long fragments

Handling Editor:  Aurélie BoninAbstract
Biomonitoring surveys make use of metabarcoding tools to describe the community 
composition These studies match their sequencing results against public genomic 
databases to identify the species However, mitochondrial genomic reference data 
are yet incomplete, only a few genes may be available, or the suitability of existing 
sequence data is suboptimal for species level resolution Here, we present a dedicated 
and cost-  effective workflow with no DNA amplification for generating complete fish 
mitogenomes for the purpose of strengthening fish mitochondrial databases Two dif -
ferent strategies using long fragment sequencing with Oxford Nanopore technology 
coupled with mitochondrial DNA enrichment were used One where the enrichment 
is achieved by preferential isolation of mitochondria followed by DNA extraction and 
nuclear DNA depletion (“mitoenrichment”) A second enrichment approach takes ad -
vantage of the CRISPR Cas9 targeted scission on previously dephosphorylated DNA 
(“targeted mitosequencing”) The sequencing results varied between tissue, species, 
and integrity of the DNA The mitoenrichment method yielded 0.17%–  12.33% of 
sequences on target and a mean coverage ranging from 74.9 to 805-  fold The tar -
geted mitosequencing experiment from native genomic DNA yielded 1.83%–  55% of 
sequences on target and a 38 to 2123-  fold mean coverage These produced complete 
mitogenomes of species with homopolymeric regions, tandem repeats, and gene rear -
rangements We demonstrate that deep sequencing of long fragments of native fish 
DNA can be achieved with low computational resources in a cost-  effective manner, 
opening the discovery of mitogenomes of nonmodel or understudied fish taxa to a 
broad range of laboratories worldwide KEYWORDS
Cas9 targeted sequencing, eDNA, fish, long fragment, mitochondrial enrichment, mitogenome
   |  819
RAMÓN-­LACA  et al 1 | INTRODUCTION
Biomonitoring surveys can provide valuable insights of the aquatic 
environment in a non-  invasive and relatively cost-  efficient manner, 
illustrating the presence-  absence and distribution of certain spe-
cies and the composition of the community of an ecosystem, and 
allow for the evaluations of these parameters as a result of variable 
conditions (Furlan et al.,  2019 ; Gallego et al., 2020 ; Stat et al., 2017 ; 
Thomsen & Willerslev, 2015 ) Besides the logistics and technical 
constraints to acquire samples, there are further challenges in ac -
curately characterizing the biome, including the molecular strategy 
used (i.e DNA extraction and the marker or gene of choice), and the 
reference databases used to identify the origin of the DNA found in 
a certain environment (Jackman et al., 2021 ; Schenekar et al., 2020 ; 
Wang et al., 2021 ) Taxonomic identification needs robust, com -
prehensive, and accurate DNA reference libraries based on solid 
taxonomic frameworks, and this effort requires more exhaustive, 
comprehensive, and revamped efforts in light of recent technical advancements in DNA sequencing (Margaryan et al., 2021 ; Novak 
et al., 2020 ; Taberlet et al., 2012) There are currently two main strategies for biomonitoring sur -
veys of environmental samples to describe the community com -
position or evaluate the abundance of certain species: (1) DNA 
metabarcoding approaches, and (2) targeted molecular assays using 
quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) or digital PCR (dPCR) 
(Shu et al., 2020 ) These molecular tools are well established and 
have been added to the toolbox of conservation management

summary: Revised Summary (Iteration 5): This article presents a cost-effective workflow for generating complete fish mitogenomes without DNA amplification. The method yielded a range of 0.17% to 12.33% on-target sequences with mean coverage ranging from 74.9 to 805-fold. It demonstrates that deep sequencing of long fragments of native fish DNA can be achieved with low computational resources, allowing for the discovery of mitogenomes of nonmodel or understudied fish taxa. This approach has the potential to strengthen fish mitochondrial databases and enable more accurate biomonitoring surveys. The study highlights the variability of sequencing results based on tissue types and emphasizes the importance of comprehensive mitochondrial genomic reference data. The research also showcases the cost-effectiveness of the method and its implications for species level resolution in taxonomic identification. Various techniques such as DNA extraction, DNA sequencing, DNA metabarcoding, quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), and digital PCR are used in biomonitoring surveys, which have implications for conservation management. This innovative method improves the efficiency and accuracy of biomonitoring surveys and contributes to the understanding of fish biodiversity and ecosystem health. The workflow allows for affordable de novo generation of fish mitogenomes, making it a valuable tool for genetic research and conservation efforts.

Chunk # 1.

Generally, these studies make use of the public genetic databases 
and, more specifically, of mitochondrial genes found in public repos -
itories to construct well represented alignments for taxa identifica -
tion purposes in the case of metabarcoding or achieve the desired 
specificity for primer design in the case of targeted assays However, 
in spite of the colossal sequencing effort undertaken in the last two 
decades with initiatives like the Barcode of Life (Mugnai et al., 2021 ; 
Ratnasingham & Hebert, 2007 ), genetic reference data for the 
breadth of taxa of interest are yet not accessible and if they are, Resumen
Los estudios de biomonitoreo utilizan herramientas de caracterización genética 
(metabarcoding) para describir la composición de la comunidad Estos estudios 
contrastan las secuencias obtenidas con bases de datos genómicas públicas para así 
identificar la especie Sin embargo, las bases de datos mitocondriales de referencia 
distan mucho de estar completas En la mayor parte de los casos solo hay unos 
pocos genes disponibles o los datos existentes no ofrecen resolución hasta el 
nivel de especie En este estudio presentamos un método dedicado a generar 
mitogenomas de peces completos de forma rentable y sin necesidad de amplificación 
del ADN, con el objeto de ampliar las bases de datos mitocondriales de peces Para 
ello se utilizaron dos enfoques diferentes de secuenciación de fragmentos largos 
utilizando secuenciación Oxford Nanopore y enriquecimiento de ADN mitocondrial 
Uno en el que el enriquecimiento se logra mediante el aislamiento preferencial 
de mitocondrias seguido de extracción del ADN y la eliminación del ADN nuclear 
(“mitoenriquecimiento”) En el segundo enfoque se aprovecha la capacidad de escisión 
dirigida por la endonucleasa CRISPR-  Cas9 sobre ADN previamente desfosforilado 
(“mitosecuenciación dirigida”) Los resultados difirieron con el tejido, la especie y la 
integridad del ADN El método de mitoenriquecimiento produjo un 0,17%–  12,33% de 
secuencias objetivo y una cobertura media entre 74,9 y 805 secuencias El experimento 
de mitosecuenciación dirigida a partir de ADN genómico nativo produjo entre 1,83 y 
55% de secuencias objetivo y una cobertura media de 38 a 2123 secuencias Este 
estudio permitió completar mitogenomas de diferentes especies que incluyen regiones 
homopoliméricas, repeticiones en tándem y reorganización de genes Demostramos 
que la secuenciación intensiva de fragmentos largos de ADN de peces es posible, se 
puede lograr con bajos recursos informáticos de una manera económica, superando el 
método generalizado de secuenciación genómica de baja cobertura y permitiendo el 
descubrimiento de mitogenomas de taxones de peces no modelo o poco estudiados a

RAMÓN-­LACA  et al only one or a few genes may be available Among these, the suitabil -
ity of existing sequence data for the purpose of designing species- 
specific oligonucleotides is typically suboptimal for targeted eDNA 
assays Such is the situation of one the most traditionally sequenced 
genes for barcoding purposes, the cytochrome c oxidase subunit 1 
(COI) of the mitochondrion that offers scattered, short conserved 
regions that are unsuitable for primer design in order to effectively 
discriminate the taxon of interest (Langlois et al., 2020 ; Margaryan 
et al., 2021 ; Schroeter et al., 2020 ) Metabarcoding analysis of fish 
targets conserved genes or regions where universal primers can be 
placed flanking interspecifically variable regions Examples are the 
12S rRNA subunit (12S) using the MiFish primers (Miya et al., 2015 ) 
that produce an amplicon of c 170 base pairs (bp) and the 16S rRNA 
(16S) with primers Ac16S that amplify a region of 330 bp (Evans 
et al., 2016 ) and c 100 bp-  fragment with Fish16S primers (Deagle 
et al., 2009 ; Shaw et al., 2016 ) The aforementioned markers have 
provided an extraordinary wealth of information for commu -
nity studies and species detection using eDNA (Miya, 2022 ; Shu 
et al., 2020 ) Building more comprehensive mitochondrial genome 
databases would be particularly advantageous for those species for 
whom identification cannot be resolved with short fragments of the 
16S, 12S, or COI genes and to expand the representation to account 
for intra and interspecific variability In addition, having more regions 
of the mitogenome available would facilitate a multi-  marker me -
tabarcoding approach, population genetics studies and even explore 
the possibility of mitochondria-  associated disorders caused by mu -
tations, an unexplored line of research that may provide insights into 
health or fitness questions (Brown, 2008 ; Dugal et al., 2021 ; Jackman 
et al., 2021 ; Jensen et al., 2020 ; Sharma & Sampath, 2019 )

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**

1. "In this study, we present a method dedicated to generating complete fish mitogenomes in a cost-effective manner without the need for DNA amplification."
2. "Two different approaches were used: long fragment sequencing using Oxford Nanopore sequencing and enrichment of mitochondrial DNA."
3. "In the first approach, enrichment was achieved by preferential isolation of mitochondria, followed by DNA extraction and removal of nuclear DNA ('mito-enrichment')."
4. "In the second approach, targeted cleavage by the CRISPR-Cas9 endonuclease was used on previously dephosphorylated DNA ('targeted mito-sequencing')."
5. "The results varied depending on the tissue, species, and DNA integrity."
6. "The mito-enrichment method produced 0.17% - 12.33% of target sequences and an average coverage of 74.9 - 805 sequences."
7. "The targeted mito-sequencing experiment using native genomic DNA produced 1.83 - 55% of target sequences and an average coverage of 38 - 2123 sequences."
8. "This study allowed the completion of mitogenomes for different species, including homopolymeric regions, tandem repeats, and gene rearrangements."
9. "Intensive sequencing of long DNA fragments in fish is possible, can be achieved with low computational resources in an economical manner, surpassing the generalized method of low-coverage genomic sequencing."
10. "This method enables the discovery of mitogenomes of non-model or understudied fish taxa in laboratories worldwide."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide detailed step-by-step protocols for the mito-enrichment and targeted mito-sequencing methods.
- The article does not specify the specific techniques or reagents used for DNA extraction, removal of nuclear DNA, or targeted cleavage.
- The article does not provide information on the specific tissues or species used in the experiments.

**Conclusion:**

The article describes a method for generating complete fish mitogenomes using two approaches: mito-enrichment and targeted mito-sequencing. The results show varying success depending on tissue, species, and DNA integrity. However, the article lacks detailed protocols and specific information on techniques and reagents used. Further information is needed to replicate the methods accurately.

summary: The studies mentioned utilize public genetic databases, specifically mitochondrial genes, to achieve taxa identification in metabarcoding and primer design for targeted assays. Despite significant sequencing efforts, comprehensive genetic reference data for the desired taxa are still inaccessible. This study presents a cost-effective method for generating complete fish mitogenomes without DNA amplification, aiming to expand the mitochondrial databases. Two different approaches were used: mitoenrichment and targeted mitosequencing. The results varied depending on tissue, species, and DNA integrity. The intensive sequencing of long DNA fragments proved feasible and economical, allowing the completion of mitogenomes for various fish species. This approach overcomes the limitations of low-coverage genomic sequencing and enables the discovery of mitogenomes for non-model or understudied fish taxa.

article:
Chunk # 2.
Despite 
the mitochondrial genes being physically linked to each other, mi -
tochondrial haplotypes from environmental DNA (eDNA) could 
determine minimum number of individuals and provide information 
about the origin of the populations (e.g., in the case of the stocks 
of anadromous fishes found in the ocean [Weitemier et al., 2021 ]) 
Additionally, whole mitochondrial genome databases, from verified, 
vouchered specimens, will also be critical for seafood monitoring, 
as molecular methods are routinely used to identify species in do -
mestic and international trade (Bourret et al., 2020 ; Ogden,  2008 ) 
The expansion of mitochondrial genomic databases is not only costly 
and requires access to voucher specimens, but also relies in the use 
of so-  called universal primers that have proven to be less generic 
than desired Whole mitochondrial sequencing, although attainable, 
is still expensive and not readily accessible for all research groups 
due to a limitation in read length, non-  affordable methods and long 
protocols (Gilpatrick et al., 2020 ) In this study, we explore target enrichment methods to attain 
whole mitogenome sequence data in a simple and cost-  effective 
manner Current technologies allow for whole genome direct se -
quencing (i.e., no DNA amplification) that can yield regions of inter -
est, particularly those in high copy number such as mitochondrial 
DNA, which can be identified and recovered from the data in a 
process called “genome skimming” or shallow sequencing (De Vivo 
et al., 2022 ; Hoban et al., 2022 ; Straub et al., 2012) We propose that targeted mitochondrial DNA enrichment during the DNA isolation 
process or library preparation step should be sought if willing to re -
duce costs, time, data storage capacity and bioinformatic capabili -
ties while improving coverage and consensus sequences This should 
avoid pseudogenes and general background noise that can affect 
the molarity of the target and thus compromise the sequencing 
performance In spite of hundreds to about a thousand mitochon -
drial DNA (mtDNA) copies present in a fish cell depending on the 
tissue and the age (lower than in mammals), the amount of mtDNA 
is normally 0.1%–  1% of the genomic DNA (Hartmann et al., 2011 ; 
Quiros et al., 2017 ; Robin & Wong, 1988 ) and is overwhelmed with 
nuclear DNA (nDNA) Different enrichment outcomes can be at -
tained depending on the DNA extraction, treatment, sequencing 
and bioinformatic approaches employed The physical properties 
of mtDNA (enclosed organelle physical location and the circularity 
of the mitochondrial genome) can be used to preferentially extract 
mtDNA using sequential precipitation methods or to deplete the 
noncircular DNA (i.e., nDNA) using exonucleases Targeted enrich -
ment can be also conducted using CRISPR-  based methods by tar -
geting conserved regions of the mitogenomes with specific guide 
RNAs (Schultzhaus et al.,  2021 ) Mitochondrial enrichment without 
PCR amplification avoids universal primer incompatibility and PCR 
amplification errors Additionally, long range amplification is prov -
ing challenging (Gilpatrick et al.,  2020 ) and target enrichment using 
hybridization capture is not yet fully operative for long fragments Long fragment sequences can help diminish the number of nu -
clear mitochondrial sequences (NumtS) that can be very long and 
are found in fish in a greater ratio than in most vertebrate species 
(Antunes & Ramos, 2005 ; Dayama et al., 2014 ) Long fragments pre -
serve the order of the genes, in contrast with the short reads se -
quencing platforms that can also be affected by PCR bias on AT-  rich 
regions (De Vivo et al., 2022 ; Gan et al., 2019 ) Long sequences can 
be key for accurate genome assembly (Pollard et al., 2018 ), in partic -
ular in repetitive regions, which are sometimes found in the control 
region of the mitochondria and have proven challenging to sequence 
with traditional methods (i.e., Sanger and whole genome sequencing 
of short fragments; Formenti et al., 2021 ; McDonald et al., 2021 ) A 
rearrangement in the order of the genes will not be missed if trans -
ferring annotations from a different species because the order of the 
genes is determined by the sequence and not the reference genome However, long-  fragment sequencing comes with its own chal -
lenges The main downside, and a common criticism, of long-  read 
sequencing with Oxford Nanopore technologies is the relatively high 
error rates in the sequences obtained Nonetheless, in opposition with 
short-  read platforms, these errors are mostly random except for the 
homopolymeric regions from single pore reads with ONT that can 
be overcome with high read depth (Pollard et al., 2018 ; Schultzhaus 
et al., 2021 ) and with the constantly evolving flow cells, chemistry and 
base calling algorithms Collections of fish have traditionally preserved 
the specimens (e.g., whole individuals, fin clips) in jars or tubes with 
>95% ethanol

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**

1. Targeted mitochondrial DNA enrichment during the DNA isolation process or library preparation step should be sought to reduce costs, time, data storage capacity, and improve coverage and consensus sequences.
2. Sequential precipitation methods can be used to preferentially extract mtDNA or deplete noncircular DNA (nDNA) using exonucleases.
3. Targeted enrichment can be conducted using CRISPR-based methods by targeting conserved regions of the mitogenomes with specific guide RNAs.
4. Mitochondrial enrichment without PCR amplification avoids universal primer incompatibility and PCR amplification errors.
5. Long fragment sequences can help diminish the number of nuclear mitochondrial sequences (NumtS) and preserve the order of the genes.
6. Long sequences are key for accurate genome assembly, particularly in repetitive regions.
7. Long-fragment sequencing with Oxford Nanopore technologies has relatively high error rates, but these errors are mostly random and can be overcome with high read depth and evolving flow cells, chemistry, and base calling algorithms.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide specific details on the sequential precipitation methods or exonucleases used for mtDNA extraction.
- The article does not provide specific details on the CRISPR-based methods used for targeted enrichment.
- The article does not provide specific details on the long fragment sequencing methods used with Oxford Nanopore technologies.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a general overview of target enrichment methods for obtaining whole mitogenome sequence data in a cost-effective manner. While it mentions specific techniques such as sequential precipitation methods, exonucleases, CRISPR-based methods, and long fragment sequencing, it does not provide detailed protocols or specific reagents used. Further information would be needed to fully implement these methods in a laboratory setting.

summary: Revised Summary: Mitochondrial haplotypes from environmental DNA (eDNA) can determine the minimum number of individuals and provide population origin information for anadromous fishes in the ocean. Whole mitochondrial genome databases, along with molecular methods, are crucial for verifying species and monitoring seafood trade. Current technologies allow for cost-effective whole mitochondrial sequencing, improving coverage and avoiding PCR amplification errors. Target enrichment methods during DNA isolation or library preparation reduce costs, improve sequencing performance, and avoid pseudogenes and background noise. Enrichment can be achieved through physical properties of mtDNA or CRISPR-based methods. Long fragment sequencing helps preserve the order of genes and aids accurate genome assembly, while short reads sequencing platforms can be affected by PCR bias in AT-rich regions. Oxford Nanopore technologies have high error rates, but these can be overcome with high read depth. Collections of fish are traditionally preserved in jars or tubes with ethanol. Various studies have explored different molecular methods for species identification and population studies.

sequencing depth while using fewer computational and sequencing 
resources than genome skimming Two approaches that enrich the 
mitochondrial DNA were evaluated in this study: (1) Mitochondrial 
DNA enrichment by isolating intact mitochondria; and (2) targeted 
mitosequencing by using CRISPR Cas9 on conserved regions of the 
mitochondrial genome Both approaches are followed by sequenc -

long-  fragment sequencing approaches efficiently produce data for 
whole mitogenomes while using less computational and sequencing 
resources than genome skimming, simplifying the discovery of mi -

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps:**

1. Two different DNA extraction methods were used: (1) an organic DNA extraction using a phenol-chloroform based method, and (2) a mitochondria isolation procedure followed by phenol-chloroform extraction.
2. A chinook salmon was euthanized, and skeletal muscle, liver, and heart samples were dissected.
3. The tissue samples were macerated using a TissueRuptor with disposable probes.
4. DNA was eluted in 100 and 50 μL of TE for the organic DNA extraction method and mitochondria isolation procedure, respectively.
5. Additional species were also extracted using either one or both methods.
6. Wide bore tips were used to avoid fragmentation.
7. All DNA extractions were performed in a dedicated genetics laboratory.
8. The quantity of DNA was quantified using the dsDNA high sensitivity or broad range kits on the Qubit.
9. For the chinook salmon, a TaqMan qPCR specific assay was used to quantify the amount of target DNA of each DNA extraction.
10. The integrity of the DNA was evaluated using the Genomic DNA ScreenTape and kit on a 2200 TapeStation System.
11. The mitochondria isolation procedure was based on sequential precipitation using the Mitochondria Isolation kit for cultured cells.
12. After isolating the mitochondria, 2 mL of mitochondria lysis buffer and 100 μL of proteinase k were added to the crude mitochondria pellet and incubated for up to 2 hours.
13. The phenol-chloroform DNA extraction was conducted.
14. Nuclear DNA (nDNA) removal was performed using a reaction with DNA, NEBuffer4, ATP, and Exonuclease V (RecBCD) to maximize the amount of pure mtDNA.

**Notes:**
- The article does not provide a complete description of the mitochondria isolation procedure. It mentions the use of a Mitochondria Isolation kit for cultured cells and a mitochondria lysis buffer, but does not provide the specific steps or concentrations used.
- The article does not provide details on the phenol-chloroform DNA extraction procedure.
- The article does not provide details on the nuclear DNA removal procedure.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some details on the DNA extraction methods used, including an organic DNA extraction method and a mitochondria isolation procedure. However, the article lacks complete information on the mitochondria isolation procedure, the phenol-chloroform DNA extraction, and the nuclear DNA removal.

summary: This study demonstrates two approaches for generating whole mitogenomes from fish species: mitochondrial DNA enrichment by isolating intact mitochondria and targeted mitosequencing using CRISPR Cas9. These approaches produce comprehensive data for whole mitogenomes while using fewer computational and sequencing resources than genome skimming. DNA extraction methods include an organic extraction and a mitochondria isolation procedure. The integrity and quantity of DNA samples were assessed using qPCR and the Genomic DNA ScreenTape kit. Mitochondria isolation involved sequential precipitation and subsequent DNA extraction. Nuclear DNA depletion was performed to maximize the amount of pure mtDNA.

topics.topic: Mitogenome generation approaches
topics.justification: The study focuses on two specific approaches for generating whole mitogenomes from fish species.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial DNA enrichment
topics.justification: One of the approaches discussed in the study involves mitochondrial DNA enrichment by isolating intact mitochondria.

topics.topic: Targeted mitosequencing using CRISPR Cas9
topics.justification: The second approach discussed in the study is targeted mitosequencing using CRISPR Cas9.

topics.topic: Comparison with genome skimming
topics.justification: The study highlights that the two approaches for generating whole mitogenomes require fewer computational and sequencing resources compared to genome skimming.

topics.topic: DNA extraction methods
topics.justification: The study mentions two DNA extraction methods: organic extraction and mitochondria isolation procedure.

topics.topic: Assessment of DNA samples
topics.justification: The integrity and quantity of DNA samples were assessed using qPCR and the Genomic DNA ScreenTape kit.

topics.topic: Mitochondria isolation procedure
topics.justification: The study explains the sequential precipitation and subsequent DNA extraction involved in the mitochondria isolation procedure.

topics.topic: Nuclear DNA depletion
topics.justification: Nuclear DNA depletion was performed to maximize the amount of pure mtDNA.

hypothetical_questions.question: How do the two approaches discussed in the study, mitochondrial DNA enrichment and targeted mitosequencing, contribute to generating whole mitogenomes from fish species?
What are the advantages of using mitochondrial DNA enrichment and targeted mitosequencing over genome skimming in terms of computational and sequencing resources?

Imagine you are a researcher working on fish species. How would you design a study using the approaches of mitochondrial DNA enrichment and targeted mitosequencing to generate whole mitogenomes while minimizing computational and sequencing resources?
How does the process of nuclear DNA depletion in the study relate to maximizing the amount of pure mtDNA for whole mitogenome generation?

As a fish enthusiast, how would you explain the significance of generating whole mitogenomes using mitochondrial DNA enrichment and targeted mitosequencing compared to other methods, such as genome skimming?

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Digestion: The digestion was performed at 37°C for 2 h followed by heat-inactivation at 70°C for 30 min.
2. DNA Cleaning and Concentration: The resulting DNA was cleaned and concentrated using 0.7× Ampure XP (Agencourt) with two washes with 70% EtOH. The DNA was eluted in 10 μL of TE (low EDTA).
3. Nanopore Library Preparation: The Nanopore library preparation was conducted with the rapid sequencing kit (SQK-RAD004, ONT). The kit uses a transposase that cleaves the DNA randomly linearizing the mitochondrial DNA while adding the necessary adapters for the motor proteins that serve as sequencing adapters. Up to 400 ng of template was used following the manufacturer's protocol.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide details about the specific enzymes used for digestion.
- The specific details of the DNA cleaning and concentration steps are not mentioned.
- The article does not provide a detailed protocol for the Nanopore library preparation, such as the specific incubation times or temperatures.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some steps for mitochondrial isolation, including digestion, DNA cleaning and concentration, and Nanopore library preparation. However, there are significant gaps in the protocol, such as the lack of specific enzyme details, incomplete information about DNA cleaning and concentration, and the absence of a detailed protocol for the library preparation.

summary:
- Digestion performed at 37°C for 2 h, followed by heat inactivation at 70°C for 30 min.
- DNA cleaned and concentrated using 0.7× Ampure XP with two washes with 70% EtOH.
- Nanopore library preparation conducted with the rapid sequencing kit (SQK-RAD004, ONT).
- Transposase used to linearize mitochondrial DNA and add necessary adapters for sequencing.
- Up to 400 ng of template used following manufacturer's protocol.
- Cas9 guide RNAs designed based on alignment of salmonid and rockfish species.
- Five candidate gRNAs selected for coverage and mismatches.
- Cutting sites on 12SrRNA, 16SrRNA, and tRNA-Gly chosen for even coverage.
- Mitogenome regions cytb and D-loop targeted for sequencing.
- Two enrichment methods: mitoenrichment and targeted mitosequencing.
- Mitochondrial DNA enriched by isolating intact mitochondria and depleting residual nuclear DNA.
- Targeted mitosequencing combines CRISPR Cas9 cutting ability with guide RNA for targeted scissions.
- DNA treated with phosphatase enzyme to prevent non-target DNA binding.
- Sequencing performed on Oxford Nanopore platform and assembly conducted.

2) These five gRNA were also tested against the whole genome of 
Sebastes schlegelii  (GenBank accession no ASM1467356) to assess 
whether the guides could target nuclear regions using a Bowtie2 
alignment within Geneious, with low sensitivity/fast parameters and 
no contigs were found The gRNA sequences were synthesized with 
a crRNA tail that will bind to the transactivating RNA (tracRNA) to 
form the duplex that the Cas9 uses to find the cutting sites (IDT) 2.4.2  |  Cas9 enrichment for mtDNA
The genomic DNA samples were treated to maximize the avail -
able mitochondrial target fragments for the sequencing process 
using the Cas9 targeted sequencing method described by Gilpatrick 
et al (2020 ) with a few variations ( Figure  1) More specifically, the 
crRNA-  tracrRNA duplexes were first assembled individually by in -
cubating 1 μL of each 100 μ M crRNA, 1 μL of 100 μ M Alt-  R CRISPR- 
Cas9 tracrRNA and 8 μL of duplex buffer (IDT) at 95°C for 5 min in separate tubes Five Alt-  R CRISPR-  Cas9 tracrRNA and guide RNA 
molecules hybridizations were subsequently combined at 10  μM 
each to form the ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes by adding 
4 μL of Alt-  R HiFi Cas9 V3 Nuclease (0.8 μL per guide included, IDT) 
and 10 μL of 10 × CutSmart buffer (New England Biolabs), 36 μ L of 
nuclease-  free H2O and incubated at room temperature for 30 min 
In the meantime, the DNA was dephosphorylated in a reaction 
that included 24 μ L genomic DNA (aiming for >210 ng μ L−1), 3 μL of 
10 × CutSmart buffer, 3 μL nuclease-  free H2O, and 3 μL Quick phos -
phatase CIP (NEB) Dephosphorylation time was increased to 20 min 
at 37°C because it proved to augment the on-  target sequences ratio 
in preliminary tests (results not shown) followed by inactivation at 
80°C for 2 min The dephosphorylated DNA sample was then sub -
jected to cleavage and dA-  tailing in a reaction that comprised 10 μL 
of Cas9 RNPs, 1 μL 10 mM dATP (NEB) and 1 μL Taq DNA polymerase 
(5000 U/mL) (NEB) The reaction was incubated at 37°C for 45 min 
followed by an inactivation at 72°C for 5 min These newly gener -
ated A-  tailed fragments were ready for ligation of the adapters using 
the LSK109 or LSK110 sequencing kits (detailed protocol in File S1 ) 2.5  |  Library sequencing
Libraries were sequenced on a MiniION Mk1C (ONT) using a FLO- 
MIN106 (R9.4.3.), FLO-  MIN111 (R10.3) or flongle FLO-  FLG001 TABLE 2  DNA extraction comparison by species, tissue type, enrichment method and integrity (DIN), total DNA quantity, target DNA

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "The genomic DNA samples were treated to maximize the available mitochondrial target fragments for the sequencing process using the Cas9 targeted sequencing method described by Gilpatrick et al (2020) with a few variations."
2. "The crRNA-tracrRNA duplexes were first assembled individually by incubating 1 μL of each 100 μM crRNA, 1 μL of 100 μM Alt-R CRISPR-Cas9 tracrRNA, and 8 μL of duplex buffer at 95°C for 5 min in separate tubes."
3. "Five Alt-R CRISPR-Cas9 tracrRNA and guide RNA molecules hybridizations were subsequently combined at 10 μM each to form the ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes by adding 4 μL of Alt-R HiFi Cas9 V3 Nuclease (0.8 μL per guide included, IDT) and 10 μL of 10× CutSmart buffer, 36 μL of nuclease-free H2O, and incubated at room temperature for 30 min."
4. "In the meantime, the DNA was dephosphorylated in a reaction that included 24 μL genomic DNA (aiming for >210 ng/μL), 3 μL of 10× CutSmart buffer, 3 μL nuclease-free H2O, and 3 μL Quick phosphatase CIP. Dephosphorylation time was increased to 20 min at 37°C followed by inactivation at 80°C for 2 min."
5. "The dephosphorylated DNA sample was then subjected to cleavage and dA-tailing in a reaction that comprised 10 μL of Cas9 RNPs, 1 μL 10 mM dATP, and 1 μL Taq DNA polymerase (5000 U/mL). The reaction was incubated at 37°C for 45 min followed by inactivation at 72°C for 5 min."
6. "These newly generated A-tailed fragments were ready for ligation of the adapters using the LSK109 or LSK110 sequencing kits."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for maximizing the available mitochondrial target fragments for sequencing using the Cas9 targeted sequencing method. The steps include assembling the crRNA-tracrRNA duplexes, combining them with Alt-R HiFi Cas9 V3 Nuclease and CutSmart buffer to form RNP complexes, dephosphorylating the DNA, cleaving and dA-tailing the DNA, and ligating the adapters. The protocol is described in sufficient detail without any gaps or uncertainties.

summary:
Iteration 6:
Summary: AliasGene, located in GenBank with accession number NC039779, was analyzed using the primer test tool and Cas9 targeted sequencing technique. The study focused on gRNA positions, specificity, mismatches, and guides within nuclear regions and genomic DNA. The sequencing process involved the use of CutSmart buffer, nuclease-free H2O, and on-target sequences. Library sequencing and DNA extraction were performed, considering species, tissue type, and integrity (DIN). The total DNA quantity, target DNA copy number, and target DNA concentration were measured, along with the ratio of target-to-total DNA. The gRNA positions (5'-3') and specificity of the gRNAs were evaluated using the primer test tool within Geneious and Bowtie2 alignment. Cas9 enrichment for mtDNA was achieved using Cas9 RNPs, Quick phosphatase CIP, Taq DNA polymerase, LSK109 or LSK110 sequencing kits, MiniION Mk1C (ONT), and FLO-MIN106 (R9.4.3.). The methods employed in the study were based on the work of Gilpatrick et al. (2020) and Iwasaki et al. The focus was on targeted sequencing of the mitogenome (mtDNA) using Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes as the enrichment method. The study involved the analysis of duplexes, on-target sequences, library sequencing, DNA extraction, integrity (DIN), target DNA copy number, target DNA concentration, and the ratio of target-to-total DNA. The study also considered the specificity of the gRNAs against the whole genome of Sebastes schlegelii and the alignment of 176 full Chondrichthyes mitogenomes.

protocol and the targeted scission of the Cas9 nuclease Among all 
the sequences obtained, a great number of complete mitogenome 
sequences were observed that were simply linearized by the Cas9 or 
the transposase enzymes Slightly lower DIN (8.9) was obtained for 
T pacificus  that were kept at −80°C for approximately a month and 
transported in dry ice for 6  h Even lower values ( <6.5) were obtained 
for M pacificus, T crenularis, D theta and S leucopsarus  that were 
kept at −80°C for a month but transported with ice blocks for 18 h 
Almost complete degradation of the DNA (DIN < 2) was observed on a T pacificus  samples that had been intermittently kept at −20°C and 
then at −80°C for more than a year ( Table  2 and Table  S2) The mitoenrichment method augmented the ratio of mitochon -
drial DNA from two-  fold in the case of heart to 26-  fold for skeletal 
muscle ( Table  2) and preserved the DNA intact with DIN ~ 7 Heart 
and liver produced more mtDNA than muscle, respectively, but also 
more nDNA and hence the increment of the proportion of target to 
total DNA is not as pronounced as muscle mitochondrial isolations 
(Table  2) Liver in combination with Longmire buffer was not useful 
as starting material for gDNA extraction: the liver sample coagulated 
and the subsequent extraction steps were difficult, resulting in an 
unattainable eluate (the DNA extract was not clean and difficult to 
pipette) Moreover, the subsequent sequencing run produced very 
few sequences compared to other runs with no clear size pattern 
despite having great amounts of DNA However, useful mtDNA was 
isolated from liver for O tshawytscha  3.2  |  Comparing mitonerichment to targeted 
mitosequencing
The total number of reads was greater on the mitoenrichment 
method, however the proportion and number of sequences on tar -
get was greater for the targeted mitosequencing method ( Table  S3, 
Figure  2) On the other hand, the read depth was more evenly dis -
tributed across the mitogenome with the mitoenrichment method 
despite the average read depth being greater for the targeted mitose -
quencing approach The use of liver increases the ratio of sequences 
on target for the mitoenrichmnent approach while also increasing 
the off-  target number of sequences, which turns into a greater use 
of the flow cell On the contrary, the heart does not perform better 
than skeletal muscle ( Table  S3, Figure  3) The more degraded the 
sample (low DIN) the lower the coverage and the shorter the reads 
were Further enrichment was achieved for samples extracted from 
isolated mitochondria and treated with the Cas9 targeted sequenc -
ing method ( Figure  3) The mitoenrichment method produced fragments of all sizes, in -
cluding full mitogenomes, because the transposase cleaves the DNA 
at random places, while the Cas9 in the targeted mitosequencing 
approach cleaves the DNA at specific sites, hence producing frag -
ments of expected sizes with all the combinations possible with one 
to five cuts ( Figure  S3.1) This can be used as an indication of success 
while monitoring the sequencing run There are typically peaks of 
sequences at ~7.5, 9 or 16 kb and the number of sequences passing 
filter on these peaks can be used as an estimation of the coverage of 
the final assembly The proportion of sequencing pores is low for these two ap -
proaches when compared to Nanopore standards, especially for 
the targeted mitosequencing method, which can be in the order of 
0.3% pores sequencing, while the mitoenrichment can have c 5% of 
sequencing pores This is due to the intense selection of mitochon -
drial DNA that drastically reduces the number of sequences available 
for each method, with only very few sequences having sequencing

summary: Revised Summary: DNA fragmentation caused by transposase activity and Cas9 nuclease scission was observed in the tshawytscha specimen. Complete mitogenome sequences were obtained through linearization by Cas9 or transposase enzymes. Different DNA degradation levels were observed based on storage conditions. The mitoenrichment method increased mitochondrial DNA ratio and preserved DNA integrity. Various species (DIN, T pacificus, M pacificus, T crenularis, D theta, and S leucopsarus) were analyzed, and different tissues (skeletal muscle, heart, and liver) were used. Liver was not suitable for gDNA extraction. Mitoenrichment had a higher total number of reads, while targeted mitosequencing had a greater proportion and number of sequences on target. Liver increased off-target sequences. The mitoenrichment method produced fragments of all sizes, while targeted mitosequencing produced fragments of expected sizes. The proportion of sequencing pores was low for both methods, with nanopore standards suggested for improvement. Optimization of sequencing runs through multiplexing/barcoding samples was recommended for future studies. Additional data can be found in Table 2 and Table S2. mtDNA and nDNA are different types of DNA. Longmire buffer is used in the extraction process. O tshawytscha is a species of interest. Relevant information is available in Table S3, Figure 2, and Figure 3. The Cas9 targeted sequencing method is used. Figure S3.1 shows nanopore standards. RAMÓN-­LACA is a researcher involved. The rapid library protocol is used for DNA preparation. Linearized total DNA is observed. Eluate and size pattern are analyzed. Read depth and coverage are important metrics. Isolated mitochondria are used in the study. Specific sites are targeted in the sequencing process. Success and failure are monitored. Optimization is needed for better results. DIN  > 9 is desired for gDNA. Nanopore standards were used for sequencing. The DNA was linearized by the Cas9 or transposase enzymes. Different samples had varying levels of DNA degradation. Liver produced more mitochondrial DNA than muscle. The mitoenrichment method resulted in a greater total number of reads. The targeted mitosequencing method had a higher proportion of sequences on target. The read depth was more evenly distributed across the mitogenome with the targeted mitosequencing approach. Liver increased the ratio of sequences on target for the mitoenrichment approach. Off-target sequences were observed. Heart did not perform better than skeletal muscle. The coverage and read length decreased with more degraded samples. Further enrichment was achieved for samples extracted from isolated mitochondria. The Cas9 targeted sequencing method produced fragments of all sizes. The transposase cleaved the DNA at random places. The Cas9 in the targeted mitosequencing approach cleaved the DNA at specific sites. Peaks of sequences were observed at ~7.5. The proportion of sequencing pores is low for these approaches. The targeted mitosequencing approach had very few sequences with sequencing adaptors. Low sequencing levels are not necessarily a failure. Future improvements should focus on optimizing sequencing runs with multiple samples.

bases (NCBI Accessions ON005612-  ON005619) The 11 consensus sequences generated for all the methods 
optimization tests for chinook salmon are between 16,628 and 
16,634 bp with 99.9% similarity, only differing in homopolymeric 
regions and follow the canonical vertebrate mitochondrial genome 
containing 37 genes (13 protein coding, 22 tRNA, and 2 rRNA genes) 
and two noncoding regions (the control region or D-  loop and the 
light strand replication origin or OL) (Figure  4) These sequences are 
10– 16 bp shorter than reference genome NC002980 (16,644 bp) 
(Wilhelm et al., 2003 ) with 112–  134 differences and a pairwise iden -
tity ranging from 99.23% (Ot-  T- H20-  mitosequencing) to 99.33% 
(Ot- T- L17 and Ot-  T- H20 mitoenrichment) The targeted mitose-
quencing run for the heart sample (Ot-  T- H20) was the lowest qual -
ity for salmon resulting in a few bp shorter and the most dissimilar 
sequence to the only reference genome available (NC002980) but 
also failed to call with confidence a short area near the cutting site of 
gRNA alias 28 that had shallow read depth (54–  60x) with the strict 
parameters given The matching sequences obtained from targeted 
mitosequencing muscle gDNA and liver mtDNA samples (Ot-  PCI- 1 
and Ot-  T- L17), with deepest coverage, was deposited in GenBank 
as the mitogenome of reference for this individual that only had 
three undetermined nucleotides out of 16,633 bp (NCBI accession 
ON005616) All the SNPs found when compared with the reference 
genome are consistent among the 11 consensus sequences from dif -
ferent tissues, samples, and protocol for the same specimen Most 
of the SNPs identified resulted in synonymous substitutions in the 
coding genes (SNPs on the third position of the codon) and no stops 
The exceptions are five nonsynonymous substitutions (Ala126Thr, 
in agreement with (Weitemier et al., 2021 ), and Ala348Thr on ND2; 
Ile152Val on COII; Leu116Glu and Met95Thr on ND6) There are 
also two one-  nucleotide insertions and two indels that cause two 
seven-  aminoacid modifications in 519-  531aa 571-  582aa of ND5 
These modifications coincide with other reference sequences for 
other salmonids (AB252719–  AB252722, AY032629–  AY032632, LC361126–  LC361129) suggesting the reference genome available in 
GenBank (NC002980) may be incorrect for this region of the ND5 
gene In addition, a previously uncharacterized ND1 aminoacid has 
been ascertained as Ala118 Despite all the above differences, most 
of the variation is found in the D-  loop with a 98.1% pairwise identity A complete mitogenome was generated for the first time for 
Merluccius productus , for which different haplotypes were found 
from four different individuals following the canonical vertebrate 
mitogenome with no gene rearrangements Three haplotypes 
were obtained from the enrichment methods (NCBI accession 
nos ON005613–  ON005615) Their length ranges from 16,736 
to 16,775 bp with the variation found in the noncoding region be -
tween genes tRNA-  Thr and tRNA-  Pro ( Figure  4) and at the end of 
the control region

summary: The flongle flow cell enabled a 2-hour targeted mitosequencing run, exhausting functional pores. In contrast, the mitoenrichment run lasted 12 hours and produced a mitogenome with 640-fold coverage. De novo assemblies of the mitogenome were generated for several species using Oxford Nanopore data. The mitogenomes of chinook salmon, Pacific hake, eulachon, blue lanterfish, California headlight fish, and Northern lampfish were successfully assembled. The generated mitogenomes have 99.9% similarity to the reference genome and contain 37 genes and two noncoding regions. The targeted mitosequencing approach has a higher proportion of on-target sequences and deeper coverage compared to mitoenrichment. However, targeted mitosequencing exhibits more variability within the mitogenome, while mitoenrichment coverage is more uniform.

summary:
Iteration 3:
Summary: The newly identified haplotypes of Atlantic hake (M merluccius) have a shorter noncoding region and a unique amino acid insertion in the ND6 gene. A complete mitogenome was generated for T pacificus, revealing a repeated motif in the control region. Tarletonbeania crenularis, Diaphus theta, and Stenobrachius leucopsarus show exceptional mitochondrial gene reorganization, including rearrangements and duplications. Diaphus theta and Stenobrachius leucopsarus have differences compared to publicly accessible references, including a putative duplication of the tRNA-Tyr gene in Merluccius merluccius (FR751402). S leucopsarus has a longer mitogenome and a tRNA gene relocation, along with a significant decrease in pairwise identity and nonsynonymous amino acid substitutions in the second half of the ND4 gene. The control region of S leucopsarus contains 44 poly-G repeats. The mitochondrial genome of T crenularis, the first publicly available, has a 321-bp insertion between the COII and tRNA-Gly genes. The research conducted by Poulsen et al (2013) using the genome skimming approach revealed novel insights into the mitochondrial genomes of these species, including variations, rearrangements, and duplications. Further investigations are needed to confirm and understand these findings.

Chunk # 10.
4 | DISCUSSION
This study evaluated simple and cost-  effective methods for the 
discovery of fish mitogenomes for the purpose of building fish mi -
tochondrial genome databases We combined long fragment se -
quencing and de novo assembly to generate unbiased consensus 
sequences that do not miss gene duplications, order variations, homopolymeric or tandem repeat regions We used two mitochon -
drial DNA enrichment approaches in combination with two different 
Oxford Nanopore sequencing kits On the one hand, the mitoenrich -
ment enhances the amount of mtDNA at the extraction and post- 
extraction steps and can be applied to all fish On the other hand, the 
targeted mitosequencing supports the sequencing of mtDNA from 
previously dephosphorylated genomic DNA with the aid of a CRISPR 
Cas9 enzyme in combination with five guide RNA sequences that 
direct the cuts With these methods, we obtained novel complete 
mitogenomes, some following the typical vertebrate mitogenome, 
some with extremely repetitive segments in the control region and 
others with gene rearrangements and indels Importantly, the success of the approach for generation of whole 
mitogenomes is predicated on the availability of high-  quality mate -
rial and proper handling, preserving the integrity and quality of high 
molecular weight DNA In this study, we used a modified phenol- 
chloroform method (Ramón-  Laca et al., 2021 ), directly to crude ly -
sate or after mitochondria isolation, that does not require pipetting 
steps or manipulation of the DNA to avoid DNA fragmentation (see 
Table  2) Preventing DNA shearing is crucial for nuclear copies of 
mitochondrial DNA (NumtS) avoidance and gene order preservation 
as well as to hamper the linearization of the circular mitochondrial 
genomes, which would decrease the amount of mtDNA at the nDNA 
depletion enrichment process High target DNA (i.e., mitochondrial 
DNA) concentrations are required to achieve the deep read coverage 
necessary to overcome the current error rates of Oxford Nanopore 
sequencing at the time of writing However, good DNA handling is 
not the only parameter that determines the DNA integrity as shown 
here with different integrity values and the wide range of sample 
preservation indicating the speed at which some fish DNA degrades 
We encourage researchers to either preserve samples in dry ice or 
at −80°C as soon as possible and to perform the extractions quickly 
after collection or to preserve the DNA from the first minute in 
Longmire buffer at room temperature; however, in this case only the 
targeted mitosequencing approach is possible because the cells and 
organelles are already lysed and the DNA is in suspension The results of the enrichment process (proportion of sequences 
on target, read depth, fragments length, method to be used) are 
heavily dependent on the type and level of degradation of the tis -
sue and thus on the quality and quantity of the DNA and enrich -
ment treatment The genome skimming results demonstrated that 
the metric of proportion of reads on target does not guarantee a 
reliable mitogenome, but the coverage and length of the fragments 
is what will determine a good result The amount of mitochondrial 
DNA found in skeletal muscle ( ~0.3% of gDNA) already showed 
some level of enrichment when compared to the expected 0.1% of 
the total DNA (Robin & Wong, 1988 ) The purification of DNA from 
isolated mitochondria enriched the proportion of target DNA by 
two in the case of heart and 26-  fold in the case of skeletal muscle 
When comparing the same chinook sample using the two enrich -
ment methods, the targeted mitosequencing approach more than 
tripled the proportion of reads on target escalating by 67 to 196-  fold

summary: Revised Summary: This study evaluated simple and cost-effective methods for discovering fish mitogenomes to build comprehensive fish mitochondrial genome databases. By combining long fragment sequencing and de novo assembly, unbiased consensus sequences were generated, capturing gene duplications, order variations, homopolymeric regions, and tandem repeat regions. Two approaches, mitoenrichment and targeted mitosequencing with CRISPR Cas9 enzyme and guide RNA sequences, were employed with Oxford Nanopore sequencing kits. The study successfully obtained novel complete mitogenomes, some exhibiting repetitive segments in the control region, gene rearrangements, and indels. Proper handling and preservation of high-quality material, including preventing nuclear copies of mitochondrial DNA (NumtS) and preserving gene order, were crucial. Preservation methods, such as the use of Longmire buffer, were recommended. The study highlighted the importance of good DNA handling and the impact of tissue degradation on the success of the enrichment process. The targeted mitosequencing approach showed higher efficiency in enriching reads on target compared to genome skimming using PCR-free genome skimming approach and Nanopore sequencing. The coverage and depth of reads were crucial indicators of successful mitogenome analysis. The study also emphasized the significance of using a modified phenol-chloroform method for DNA extraction to preserve DNA integrity and avoid fragmentation. Different tissues and extraction methods were compared, and the use of Nanopore sequencing for genome skimming analysis was explored. The coverage and depth of reads were found to be crucial indicators of successful mitogenome analysis. Previous studies by Ramón-Laca, Robin & Wong, De Vivo et al., and Franco-Sierra & Díaz-Nieto have contributed to the field of mitogenome analysis.

article:
Chunk # 11.
The deep coverage results 
obtained for targeted mitosequencing are, not surprisingly, remark -
ably higher than other Cas9 targeted assays on nuclear regions of 
interest Gilpatrick et al  (2020 ) obtained a coverage of 80 × for a 
nuclear gene with similar length as the mitogenomes (18 kbp), but 
680× when targeting the region with multiple cuts Since the num -
ber of available mitochondrial copies is up to 500 times the number 
of nuclear copies the results are comparable to the latter mentioned 
study The cutting sites for the targeted mitosequencing approach 
are notably less covered than the rest of the mitogenome (File S3.3), 
but these sites are precisely the least variable regions of the mitog -
enome among fish Directional bias is created at the cut site, due to 
the retention of bound Cas9 ribonucleotide complexes on cleaved 
DNA fragments that are distal to the PAM site (shown on the wide 
coverage range for mitosequencing in Figure  3) While Sanger and mass parallel sequencing of short reads can 
hinder and obscure challenging tandem repeat regions found in the 
noncoding regions of the mtDNA of some organisms, such as the 
tandem repeat insertion in the control region of eulachon or the 
44 bp stretch of polyG found on S leucopsarus , in this study (Filipović 
et al., 2021 ; Kinkar et al., 2020 ), long read sequencing can overcome 
this obstacle We expect a few scattered errors in the homopoly -
meric regions in the mitogenomes generated herein as shown in De 
Vivo et al (2022 ) (sequencing error rates were ≥2% at the time of 
this study with Guppy5sup basecalling and R9.4 flow cells), but these 
are to be fixed as the base calling algorithms evolve or ultimately 
with the new sequencing kits and flow cells that increase yield and 
accuracy and allow for sequencing speed control (SQK-  LSK114 and 
flow cell R10.4) that were just released at the time of finalizing this 
manuscript, which were shown to reach >99% accuracy These im -
provements will presumably allow single nucleotide variants and 
possible heteroplasmy detection (Keraite et al., 2022 ) On the other 
hand, the genomes generated in this study are likely to be more 
accurate than previous efforts since the methods overcomes the 
difficulty of sequencing the tandem repeats and long insertions, 
repetitions and rearrangements that can take place in vertebrate 
genomes (Formenti et al., 2021 ), provide read depth, favour long 
fragments of DNA and augment the proportion of the target To cir -
cumvent sequencing errors, we recommend read depth of 50–  75× 
for reliable mitogenome generation at the current error rate to get 
accurate mitogenomes with R9.4 flow cells Srivathsan et al (2021 ) demonstrate that 25–  50× for R10.3 flow cells is sufficient and this 
can only decrease with future improvements The mitoenrichment method can be used straight away from 
mitochondrial material of potentially any species and is particularly 
useful in the absence of a close reference mitogenome However, 
there can be nDNA copurification to some extent Exonuclease V 
digestion helps reduce the noise caused by the nDNA, yet incom -
plete The rapid sequencing kit (ONT) uses a transposase and a 
rapid adapter ligation that makes the library preparation brief and 
straightforward We speculate that this method will also work on 
any phyla provided there is enough mitochondrial representation 
of the target vs microorganisms (i.e., little contamination from sym -
bionts or gut microbiota) as seen in preliminary work performed 
on bivalves (Ramón-  Laca, personal observation) On the contrary, 
the Cas9 guides RNAs developed for the targeted mitosequencing 
method will be useful for most fish and particularly the guides that 
cut the 12S and 16SrRNA genes The two guides located on the 
tRNA-  Gly may be less universal since this region is not as conserved 
and there may be mismatches Nevertheless, both methods may not 
be applicable for organism for which minute amounts of tissue are 
available Both methods take the same time, around 2.5 h for the 
library preparation from DNA treatment after the DNA extraction 
to library load on the flow cell, but the mitoenrichment includes an 
extra step prior to the DNA extraction to isolate the mitochondria The targeted mitosequencing approach tends to shorten the 
life of the regular flow cells and particularly so in the case of the 
flongle compared to the mitoenrichment method, presumably due 
to the presence of the dephosphorylated DNA that may be block -
ing pores

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The mitoenrichment method can be used straight away from mitochondrial material of potentially any species and is particularly useful in the absence of a close reference mitogenome.
2. Exonuclease V digestion helps reduce the noise caused by the nDNA, yet incomplete.
3. The rapid sequencing kit (ONT) uses a transposase and a rapid adapter ligation that makes the library preparation brief and straightforward.
4. We speculate that this method will also work on any phyla provided there is enough mitochondrial representation of the target vs microorganisms (i.e., little contamination from symbionts or gut microbiota).
5. The Cas9 guides RNAs developed for the targeted mitosequencing method will be useful for most fish and particularly the guides that cut the 12S and 16SrRNA genes.
6. The two guides located on the tRNA-Gly may be less universal since this region is not as conserved and there may be mismatches.
7. Both methods may not be applicable for organisms for which minute amounts of tissue are available.
8. Both methods take the same time, around 2.5 h for the library preparation from DNA treatment after the DNA extraction to library load on the flow cell, but the mitoenrichment includes an extra step prior to the DNA extraction to isolate the mitochondria.
9. The targeted mitosequencing approach tends to shorten the life of the regular flow cells and particularly so in the case of the flongle compared to the mitoenrichment method, presumably due to the presence of the dephosphorylated DNA that may be blocking pores.

**Conclusion:** The article provides information on the mitoenrichment method for mitochondrial isolation, including the use of exonuclease V digestion to reduce nDNA noise and the use of a rapid sequencing kit for library preparation. However, specific steps for the mitoenrichment method are not provided, and there is no mention of the isolation protocol for the mitochondria. The article also mentions the development of Cas9 guide RNAs for targeted mitosequencing and notes that both methods may not be applicable for organisms with limited tissue availability. Additionally, it highlights the impact of the targeted mitosequencing approach on the lifespan of flow cells. Overall, while the article provides some information on the methods used, it lacks a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation.

summary: Summary 4: The targeted mitosequencing approach, utilizing Cas9, provides deep coverage and accurate results for analyzing nuclear genes and mitochondrial regions of interest. The Cas9 ribonucleotide complexes bind to the PAM site, creating directional bias and cleaved DNA fragments. Sanger and mass parallel sequencing generate short reads, while long read sequencing captures homopolymeric regions. The study focuses on eulachon, highlighting tandem repeat insertion and polyG regions. Sequencing error rates are considered, and new sequencing kits with increased yield and accuracy are used. Read depth is crucial for reliable mitogenome generation, and R10.3 flow cells are suitable for sequencing. The method is applicable to various phyla, and preliminary work has been done on Cas9 guides RNAs. The presence of dephosphorylated DNA may shorten the life of the flow cells. The study also addresses the importance of nDNA copurification, exonuclease V digestion, and the use of a rapid sequencing kit. Heteroplasmy detection and accurate mitogenomes are key outcomes of this research.

article:
Chunk # 12.
On the other hand, the mitoenrichment method worked 
well on a flongle making it an ideal approach for de novo sequencing 
that will reduce the cost per samples if running them individually 
(67 USD per mitogenome without labour and reagents) compared 
to the price of a regular flow cell (116 USD per mitogenome, se -
quencing cost without labour and reagents) (De Vivo et al.,  2022 ) 
However, a refrigerated centrifuge is still needed for the mitochon -
dria isolation and it comes with the disadvantage of the flongle 
flow cells having a very short shelf-  life, which reduces the chances 
of improvisation, especially in remote places All the analyses of 
this study were carried out on a desktop computer using standard 
analysis software within Geneious Primer 2021, except for the 
Guppy5sup base calling that was performed using Google Colab 
with a subscription of 10 USD a month, which allowed the use of 
a high-  end GPU that completed basecalling at a rate >  106 bp s−1 
At the time of these experiments a barcoding kit or protocol for 
mitosequencing of multiple samples was not available and it was 
more practical to carry out each mitogenome sequencing in a dif -
ferent run to guarantee good results and reuse the flow cell up 
to five times Keraite et al (2022 ) ran multiple samples by using 
different guide RNAs for each sample of human origin However, 
this is less attainable with de novo sequencing of mitogenomes of 
fishes given their diversity but could be useful for multiple sam -
ples of the same species Comparatively, the widespread genome

could be decreased to USD 28 if pooling 1000 samples and aiming 
for 100x on target read depth (Margaryan et al., 2021 ) However, 
this approach is only worth if pooling many different samples, 
which entails time and a great effort that is likely to involve the 
coordination of many different institutions and archived samples 
that may see the sample quality compromised In addition, intense 
computational power would be necessary for genome skimming, 
especially if aiming for deep read approaches to gain coverage that 
can produce 5 Gb per specimen (Margaryan et al., 2021 ), which 
may be impractical for many researchers and laboratories, but 
also may take weeks to process while the approaches shown here 
can take 2 days from DNA extraction to mitogenome annotation 
Moreover, typical sequencing lengths of short-  fragment platforms 
range from 150–  300 bp and thus this approach is expected to be 
more prone to include nuclear copies of the mitochondria (Numts) 
in the contig, resulting in illegitimate or inaccurate sequences 
However, here, we have proven that in the event of the DNA being 
degraded (e.g., eulachon Table  S3), genome skimming is the only 
alternative in which case the researcher will have to assume the 
potential contamination with Numts that may be overcome by a 
high proportion of mtDNA that is likely to be due to degradation of 
linear DNA This would be a more suitable approach for archived 
samples from museums The targeted mitosequencing approach can be further optimized 
by introducing some of the features used by Keraite et al  (2022 ) They 
pre- enriched by selecting the circular DNA using exonuclease V like 
we did here for the mitoenrichment approach with a 27%–  49% of 
reads increase (useful for high integrity DNA only) and they also per -
formed a digestion with proteinase K after the cleavage of the DNA 
to avoid the directional bias seen in our mitosequencing workflow 
from bound Cas9 enzymes to the DNA that increase the yield of full 
length reads two-  fold Further targeted enrichment could possibly be 
attained at the sequencing step without any further laboratory-  bench 
effort by using the adaptive sampling capability of the nanopores 
(Martin et al., 2021 ; Payne et al., 2021 ) This process requires refer -
ence sequence of the target or related species though We speculate 
that if all fish mitogenomes are uploaded as references to enrich for, 
the on-  target yield could be improved However, this tool at the se -
quencing level requires greater computational power and would need 
to be validated to ensure it does not bias the results Alternative meth -
ods to enrich the mitochondrial DNA yield could also be pursued using 
custom myBaits mito targeted sequencing kit (Arbor Biosciences)

summary: The mitoenrichment method is effective for de novo sequencing, reducing costs compared to regular flow cells. However, it requires a refrigerated centrifuge and has a short shelf-life. The study used standard analysis software, except for base calling in Google Colab. Barcoding kits were not available, so each mitogenome was sequenced separately. Genome skimming on a NovaSeq platform is another option, but pooling many samples and coordinating institutions is necessary. It requires intense computational power and may take weeks to process. Short-fragment platforms are prone to including nuclear copies of mitochondria, while degraded DNA requires genome skimming. The targeted mitosequencing approach can be optimized by pre-enrichment and digestion with proteinase K. Adaptive sampling with nanopores can improve on-target yield but requires reference sequences. Alternative methods include using custom myBaits mito targeted sequencing kit.

However, this is a less universal, more complex and longer procedure 
(Zascavage et al., 2019 ) Instead, size selection could be achieved with 
gel extraction (e.g., BluePippin platform), but this instrument was not 
available in this study and it was therefore not tested A great potential advantage of the MinION sequencing plat -
form and associated single flow cell Oxford Nanopore sequencers is 
the portability outside of a traditional laboratory The MinION has 
great portability and the instruments and equipment necessary (e.g., 
laptop, pipettes, minicentrifuge, small thermal cycler, vortex, plas -
ticware) could also be easily transported in a couple of suitcases In fact, the experiments in this study were mostly undertaken using a 
portable setup in a home office during the beginning of the Covid19 
pandemic showing that the laboratory requirements can be kept to 
a minimum Although some of the reagents and the flow cells listed 
in the methods section would be more limiting as they need to be 
stored either in the fridge or freezer, these are often readily acces -
sible on research vessels and at remote field stations, or portable 
refrigeration could be used In the field, the targeted mitosequenc -
ing approach could be modified to be performed in situ, replacing 
the phenol-  chloroform DNA purification with a simpler HMW DNA 
extraction (e.g., Monarch HMW kit, New England Biolabs), with very 
little equipment The mitoenrichment approach uses the Rapid se -
quencing kit SQK-  RAD004 (ONT) The field sequencing kit (SQK- 
LRK001) is fundamentally the same, but the chemistry is dehydrated 
to allow its use in the field in the absence of a freezer (De Vivo 
et al., 2022 ) The portability of this approach affords the opportunity 
not only to take these resources to the field, but in doing so, takes 
advantages of the freshness of samples to yield optimal results, and 
the production of data in a timely manner In addition to supplementing public databases with the standard 
metabarcoding mitochondrial genes (e.g., 12S, 16S, COI), there are 
other genes (e.g., D-  loop) that may be relevant to those species with 
recent evolutionary histories In addition, the complete mitogenome 
availability will enhance multiple marker metabarcoding efforts 
(Leite et al., 2021 ), providing the resources for population mitog -
enomics (e.g., hypervariable noncoding region found in this study 
among three different individuals of Pacific hake) and will provide 
insightful knowledge about radiation and evolutionary processes 
with possible gene rearrangement With deeper sequencing and re -
search, it may even help gain perspective about mitochondrial disor -
ders and diseases of wildlife In testing these methods for mitogenome sequencing, we gen -
erated new reference sequences for several species: a new refer -
ence mitogenome for chinook salmon ( Oncorhynchus tshawytscha ) 
and complete mitogenomes for two key species of economical and 
conservation importance, the Pacific hake ( Merluccius productus ) 
and eulachon ( Thaleichthys pacificus ) Additionally, the complete 
mitogenome of three myctophid fishes were generated for the 
first time in this study: the blue lanternfish ( Tarletonbeania crenu -
laris), Northern lampfish ( Stenobrachius leucopsarus ), and California 
headlighfish ( Diaphus theta ) Myctophids (lanterfishes and black -
chins) are a common finding in our laboratory metabarcoding 
studies from oceanic samples, being the most abundant family 
of fishes in the oceans in terms of biomass (Poulsen et al., 2013 ; 
Saunders et al., 2019 ) Myctophiformes is a very diverse fam -
ily with 250 species described with very divergent larval forms 
that is far from complete in the molecular databases (Poulsen 
et al., 2013 ) Moreover, very few specimens remain identifiable 
in survey net hauls because their scales and photophores pattern 
are often damaged Getting more voucher specimens sequenced 
is thus important and de novo assembly of this group of fishes 
is key because the gene rearrangement made mapping to a close 
relative mitogenome insufficient, resulting in shorter genomes

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "A great potential advantage of the MinION sequencing platform and associated single flow cell Oxford Nanopore sequencers is the portability outside of a traditional laboratory."
2. "The MinION has great portability and the instruments and equipment necessary (e.g., laptop, pipettes, minicentrifuge, small thermal cycler, vortex, plasticware) could also be easily transported in a couple of suitcases."
3. "Although some of the reagents and the flow cells listed in the methods section would be more limiting as they need to be stored either in the fridge or freezer, these are often readily accessible on research vessels and at remote field stations, or portable refrigeration could be used."
4. "In the field, the targeted mitosequencing approach could be modified to be performed in situ, replacing the phenol-chloroform DNA purification with a simpler HMW DNA extraction (e.g., Monarch HMW kit, New England Biolabs), with very little equipment."
5. "The mitoenrichment approach uses the Rapid sequencing kit SQK-RAD004 (ONT)."
6. "The field sequencing kit (SQK-LRK001) is fundamentally the same, but the chemistry is dehydrated to allow its use in the field in the absence of a freezer."
7. "In addition to supplementing public databases with the standard metabarcoding mitochondrial genes (e.g., 12S, 16S, COI), there are other genes (e.g., D-loop) that may be relevant to those species with recent evolutionary histories."
8. "In addition, the complete mitogenome availability will enhance multiple marker metabarcoding efforts, providing the resources for population mitogenomics and will provide insightful knowledge about radiation and evolutionary processes with possible gene rearrangement."
9. "With deeper sequencing and research, it may even help gain perspective about mitochondrial disorders and diseases of wildlife."
10. "In testing these methods for mitogenome sequencing, we generated new reference sequences for several species: a new reference mitogenome for chinook salmon (Oncorhynchus tshawytscha) and complete mitogenomes for two key species of economical and conservation importance, the Pacific hake (Merluccius productus) and eulachon (Thaleichthys pacificus)."
11. "Additionally, the complete mitogenome of three myctophid fishes were generated for the first time in this study: the blue lanternfish (Tarletonbeania crenularis), Northern lampfish (Stenobrachius leucopsarus), and California headlighfish (Diaphus theta)."
12. "Myctophids (lanterfishes and black-chins) are a common finding in our laboratory metabarcoding studies from oceanic samples, being the most abundant family of fishes in the oceans in terms of biomass."
13. "Myctophiformes is a very diverse family with 250 species described with very divergent larval forms that is far from complete in the molecular databases."
14. "Getting more voucher specimens sequenced is thus important and de novo assembly of this group of fishes is key because the gene rearrangement made mapping to a close relative mitogenome insufficient, resulting in shorter genomes with missing genes."
15. "In summary, the targeted mitosequencing and mitoenrichment approaches, paired with the portable MinION sequencer, gives rapid, cost-effective results for the generation of whole mitogenomes, which are increasingly important for explorations of biological diversity in environmental studies."

**Conclusion:** The article discusses the advantages of using the MinION sequencing platform for mitochondrial isolation, highlighting its portability and the minimal equipment required. summary: The MinION sequencing platform offers portable and efficient size selection for sequencing. The experiments in this study were conducted using a portable setup during the Covid-19 pandemic. The field sequencing kit allows for in situ sequencing with minimal equipment. The portability of this approach enables timely data production. The availability of complete mitogenomes enhances population genomics and provides insights into evolutionary processes. New reference sequences were generated for several species, including chinook salmon, Pacific hake, and eulachon. Myctophid fishes, a diverse family, were sequenced for the first time, contributing to the understanding of their molecular diversity. The targeted mitosequencing and mitoenrichment approaches, combined with the portable MinION sequencer, yield rapid and cost-effective results for whole mitogenome generation, crucial for studying biological diversity in environmental research.

topics.justification: The field sequencing kit allows for in situ sequencing with minimal equipment, which is a significant advantage mentioned in the summary.

topics.topic: Timely data production enabled by portability
topics.justification: The portability of the approach enables timely data production, emphasizing the importance of quick results in the context of the study.

topics.topic: Enhanced population genomics with complete mitogenomes
topics.justification: The availability of complete mitogenomes enhances population genomics and provides insights into evolutionary processes, which is a key outcome mentioned in the summary.

topics.topic: Generation of new reference sequences for species
topics.justification: New reference sequences were generated for several species, including chinook salmon, Pacific hake, and eulachon, indicating the contribution to species-specific research and understanding.

topics.topic: Molecular diversity of Myctophid fishes
topics.justification: Myctophid fishes, a diverse family, were sequenced for the first time, contributing to the understanding of their molecular diversity, which is a significant finding mentioned in the summary.

topics.topic: Rapid and cost-effective whole mitogenome generation
topics.justification: The targeted mitosequencing and mitoenrichment approaches, combined with the portable MinION sequencer, yield rapid and cost-effective results for whole mitogenome generation, highlighting the efficiency and cost-effectiveness of the method.

topics.topic: Importance of studying biological diversity in environmental research
topics.justification: Whole mitogenome generation is crucial for studying biological diversity in environmental research, emphasizing the relevance and significance of the study in the broader field of environmental research.

Mitogenomes can be generated ad 
hoc on one to a few samples at a time with little computational ef -
fort Future improvements on samples multiplexing or in sequencing 
devices will enable high-  throughput sequencing of mitogenomes at 
once The methods outlined here rely heavily on high quality, HMW 
DNA We strongly recommend the targeted mitosequencing using 
the gRNAs designed in this study for all bony fishes, whereas the mi -
toenrichment would be preferred on distant taxa for which a group- 
specific targeted sequencing has not been developed Genome 
skimming is recommended when only degraded DNA is available 
(e.g., museum specimens in ethanol) We believe these approaches 
will make the generation of reference mitogenomes accessible to

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "Future improvements on samples multiplexing or in sequencing devices will enable high-throughput sequencing of mitogenomes at once."
2. "The methods outlined here rely heavily on high quality, HMW DNA."
3. "We strongly recommend the targeted mitosequencing using the gRNAs designed in this study for all bony fishes."
4. "The mitoenrichment would be preferred on distant taxa for which a group-specific targeted sequencing has not been developed."
5. "Genome skimming is recommended when only degraded DNA is available (e.g., museum specimens in ethanol)."
6. "We believe these approaches will make the generation of reference mitogenomes accessible to many researchers worldwide."

**Conclusion:** The article provides general recommendations and approaches for generating mitogenomes, including targeted mitosequencing, mitoenrichment, and genome skimming. However, it does not provide a detailed protocol or step-by-step instructions for mitochondrial isolation.
